segs.utf8:
I’m Justin Rowlatt, the BBC’s climate editor 
and I’m going to be explaining how heat pumps,

and fridges and air conditioners, really work.
 
This is a heat pump and it does something that

appears miraculous. For every unit of energy 
you put in here, you can get as much three,

maybe even four units of heat out which makes it 
three hundred, four hundred per cent efficient.

Now you are thinking how is that even 
possible – that’s why I am here to explain.

Now if you’re thinking a heat pump looks a 
bit like an air conditioner that’s because

it is. Heat pumps, air conditioners, fridges 
and freezers all do the same thing - they

move the heat from one place to another.
 
There’s nothing new about the technology. It

has been around for years. The first fridge was 
invested by this man, Jacob Perkins, a British

settler in the US, almost 200 years ago (1834).
So how do heat pumps do it? You may have heard

people say they work like a fridge in reverse but 
be honest with me, you don’t know how a fridge

actually works do you? So here’s the science bit.
Buckle up because University College London have

given me access to their fabulous laboratories 
to take you on a journey into some of the

weirder properties of matter and some of the 
fundamental laws of the universe as we dive into

the science of the heat pump.
 
 
And to get things started,

let’s look under the hood…
 
Looks pretty complicated, doesn’t

it? But it’s actually quite simple when you know 
how it works. All you need to know at the moment

is this network of pipes is all sealed up and it 
contains a magic fluid called a refrigerant. The

refrigerant is key to how the whole thing works.
 
Now, back to the fundamental

laws of the universe.
 
Heat pumps rely on the second

law of thermodynamics to operate. That decrees 
that heat always flows from something hot like

me to something cooler like the air all around 
me. It’s why your coffee gets cold and why ice

melts. Heat pumps use this law to move energy, 
move heat from the outside into your home.

They can do it even when the air is 
literally freezing outside – sub-zero.

And they do it so effectively because they 
exploit a very peculiar quality of matter,

something that happens at what is known as 
the phase change. So, what is a phase change.

What is a phase change?
 
A phase change is when matter

changes state so it goes from solid to liquid, 
liquid to gas – or the back the other way again.

Something weird happens to materials during a 
phase change – energy you put in stops heating

them up. Or more precisely the energy you put 
in stops making them hotter and goes into making

phase change happen. What do I mean by that? 
 
Let’s take a kettle and fill it up with water.

So when your kettle is boiling away, energy is 
going into breaking the bonds that hold the water

in a watery state. It’s not getting any hotter but 
it’s got whole lot more energy in it. That energy,

that stored energy is known as latent heat.  
 
Materials can store a lot of latent heat as they

go through a phase change. And it’s all that 
energy stashed away that heat pumps use.

So we have seen how much energy it takes to boil 
water but lots of other materials turn into gases

at a much lower temperature. Like this stuff – 
this is dry ice. This is frozen carbon dioxide.

Look at this. Put them in here…wow. So dry ice 
turns into gas, into carbon dioxide at -78C. You

can see it turning into a gas before our eyes.
 
Why do these materials like dry ice turn into

gas at such low temperatures? Because the bonds 
between the molecules are much weaker. With water,

the bonds are much stronger – which 
means the boiling point is much higher.

Heat pumps use all sorts of materials as 
refrigerants. Some use materials called

HFCs. Back in the old days – they used 
CFCs. But both of those are quite damaging

to the atmosphere so increasingly they are 
using more commonplace materials – natural

refrigerants. Some use propane. 
 
 
Some use carbon dioxide. I’m going to demonstrate

using butane in this can – which is very similar 
to propane. And I got a thermometer here to show

you something rather special. Butane is a liquid 
in a can. But its boiling point is -36C. Release

it from the can. Get it boiling. And you can see 
the temperature goes down, in fact, to -40.

So think about it. There is a load of heat even 
on a very cold day, even when it’s sub zero,

to get the liquid boiling to draw in 
all that energy, all that heat.

Let’s take a closer look at our heat pump. Now 
this bit down here… This is the “evaporator”.

That’s where the refrigerant is sprayed 
out into the network of pipes at the back.

So it’s -36C when it’s released, you remember. 
 
Take a look over here. You got a fan. What a fan

does is to move the air over the pipe giving 
it lots of opportunity to do what the second

law of thermodynamics says it wants to do and 
move from warm to cold. It’s warming up the

refrigerant and turning it into gas. And if you 
follow the pipe – with the refrigerant now a gas

with its molecules all bouncing energetically 
around. It moved down to this bit, the pump bit

of a heat pump. This is the compressor. It does 
what the name suggests and compresses the gas.

What happens when you put our refrigerant 
under pressure? You’ve probably experienced

something similar when you pump up the tyres 
on a bike. Your pump gets hot, doesn’t it?

(
 
We can demonstrate

that with this. We got a tiny bit of cotton 
wool at the bottom. And effectively what we

got here is a piston. I put that in there…. Like 
that. Lights off please, Miho. And look at this.

What is happening here. When you push down the 
piston and it concentrates the air in here. The

molecules get closer together, don’t they. They 
are interacting more and are bumping against each

other more. That raises the temperature.  
 
And the pump in the heat pump does the

same thing. It raises the temperature of the 
refrigerant releasing the heat that it’s harvested

from outside. Now the challenges to get that 
heat from the refrigerant into your home.

And that is done by this bit of the heat pump. 
This is the condenser. So the hot refrigerant in

this pipe passes through another pipe that 
contains the cold water from your heating

system. Quiz time - what does the second law 
of thermodynamics decree will happen? Yes, the

heat from the refrigerant passes into your heating 
system giving you a warm home or a hot shower.

Which sounds like the end of the story, doesn’t 
it. Except I’ve missed out one crucial property

of matter these clever machines exploit.
 
This last property relies on the fact that

the boiling point is not just 
about the temperature.

Let me show you. So you got a flask full of 
water here. I got a pump which I’m turning

on. The pump is going to suck the air out of 
the flask. We are going to see what happens

to the water which is at a room temperature. As 
I release the valve….. sucking the air out of

the vessel. Look at that. It begins to boil. 
 
So what’s happening. If you think about it. In

the atmosphere above us, there are loads of 
molecules stuck on top of each other pressing

down on the pan of water. Lift away those 
molecules and create a vacuum as I have here,

and it begins to boil much more easily. I 
can check the temperature. And it is 18C.

So it is basically room temperature. 
 
 
We can experience this without creating

a vacuum. Picture Sir Edmund Hillary and Sherpa 
Tensing up on the top of Mount Everest. They stop

to make a cuppa. They put a pan on the gas stove 
but, because the pressure is so much lower on top

of the Everest, the water doesn’t boil at 100C but 
at more like 68C which gives them a problem.

That isn’t hot enough to draw the flavour 
from the tea. The water needs to be more

than 90C to do that. But they do have one 
advantage though. It means they can drink

their tea straight from the pan because 
we normally drink tea at about 65C.

Now let’s look at the other side. Let’s 
look at, instead of lowering the pressure,

raising the pressure and what happens then. I 
have here a pressure cooker. What the pressure

cooker does, as the name suggests… is as water 
boils it traps the water vapour and it pushes

down on the liquid making it harder for it to 
boil and raises the boiling point. We can see

the pressure here. You can also see that 
the temperature is higher than the normal

boiling point. So it is 114C. Instead 
of the 100C. That’s why the extra heat,

that’s why your food cooks more quickly in a 
pressure cooker. And it also uses less energy.

So why is pressure so important. 
Let’s go back to the butane.
Remember it is liquid under pressure at
room temperature. Spray it out…

It gets very cold, expands and draws in heat. 
That gas now full of latent heat goes into a

compressor . It’s pressed down and the temperature 
raises. We put it in the condenser. The

refrigerant releases its heat into your water into 
your heating system so now it’s cooler. And it’s

also under pressure. And it becomes liquid. There 
is one more process you need to know about.

The last bit of that heat pump we need to 
know about is the expansion valve. It’s

somewhere in here. I don’t know where it is. 
To be honest, it really doesn’t matter. It

does what its name suggests. It expands and 
eases the pressure off allowing the liquid to

expand. We know what happens when we release the 
pressure. It gives molecules more room to move,

doesn’t its it. They bump into each other 
less and they cool down. Now we have cool

liquid refrigerants ready to go back into the 
evaporator and start the whole process again.

Evaporating… Collecting the heat from the air… 
Concentrating it in the pump. Releasing it into

the hot water in the system giving you a warm 
home or a hot shower. BINGO a heat pump.

And all super-efficient. Remember one unit of 
energy in, three, four, some modern heat pumps,

five units of heat out. And fridges and 
air conditioners? They work in reverse,

don’t they – they take heat out of 
a space and moving it outside.
So now you know how heat pumps, fridges,

freezers and air conditioners all 
work. Feel free to share the good news!
